lessons.title: Three-Way Comparison: the spaceship operator <=>
lessons.motivation: Before C++20, fully ordering a user-defined type required six operators: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`. All six are nearly identical and must stay in sync — a classic source of bugs and duplication. For heterogeneous comparison (e.g., `MyString` vs `const char*`) the count doubled. The `<=>` ("spaceship") operator (P0515) eliminates the burden: one definition replaces the whole set.
lessons.theory:
Before C++20, fully ordering a type required six comparison operators written by hand and kept in sync. The `<=>` operator (spaceship operator, P0515) returns not a `bool` but a *comparison category*: negative if `a < b`, zero if `a == b`, positive if `a > b`. From a single `operator<=>` the compiler automatically derives `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`; from a single `operator==` it derives `!=`.

C++20 defines three comparison categories. `std::strong_ordering` (integers) is a strict total order: if `a == b`, the objects are indistinguishable. Values: `less`, `equal`, `greater`. `std::weak_ordering` (e.g., case-insensitive string comparison) is a total order where equivalent objects may still differ in other respects. Values: `less`, `equivalent`, `greater`. `std::partial_ordering` (floating point) is used when not all pairs are comparable: `NaN` is unordered relative to everything. Values: `less`, `equivalent`, `greater`, `unordered`. The hierarchy: `strong_ordering` implicitly converts to `weak_ordering`, which in turn converts to `partial_ordering`.

`= default` for `<=>` generates lexicographic member-wise comparison in declaration order. The result type is determined automatically as the weakest category among all member types. A defaulted `<=>` also causes the compiler to synthesize `operator==` (lexicographic), so a single `= default` gives all six operators for a value-type struct.

Comparisons in C++20 are reversible: `a == b` is also tried as `b == a`; `a < b` can be derived from both `a <=> b` and `b <=> a`. This means one `operator<=>` is sufficient for heterogeneous comparison. All standard library types — `std::string`, `std::vector`, `std::optional`, and others — gained `<=>` support in C++20.

examples.title: Versions and case-insensitive strings
examples.expectedOutput:
v1 < v2: true
v1 > v2: false
v1 == v3: true
v1 <= v3: true
Hello == hello: true
Hello < World: true
examples.explanation: `Version` uses `= default` — the compiler generates lexicographic comparison over `major`, `minor`, `patch` and all six operators automatically. `CaseInsensitiveString` defines `weak_ordering` manually because `"Hello"` and `"hello"` are equivalent in ordering but store different data.

examples.title: Comparison categories and sorting
examples.expectedOutput:
int <=> yields strong_ordering
converts to weak:    less
converts to partial: less
NaN <=> 1.0 unordered: true

Sorted by salary:
  Bob: 75000
  Alice: 90000
  Charlie: 90000
examples.explanation: `strong_ordering` (result of `int <=> int`) implicitly converts to both `weak_ordering` and `partial_ordering`. `double` yields `partial_ordering`, and `NaN <=> 1.0` gives `unordered`. `Employee` with `weak_ordering` by salary sorts correctly with `std::sort`: Alice and Charlie are equivalent in order but remain distinct objects.
